text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY CONSULTATION NOTE

PATIENT: Raymond Kowalski
ATTENDING ONCOLOGIST: [Attending Oncologist]
REFERRING PHYSICIAN: [Referring Gastroenterologist/Surgeon]
CONSULTATION TYPE: New Patient – Medical Oncology
ECOG PERFORMANCE STATUS: 1

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Mr. Kowalski is a 58-year-old Polish-American male with a recent diagnosis of Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases, referred to medical oncology for treatment planning and multidisciplinary coordination.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Kowalski presented to his primary care physician approximately six weeks ago with a several-month history of altered bowel habits, intermittent hematochezia, and mild left lower quadrant cramping. He noted progressive constipation alternating with looser stools over a period of roughly four to five months, which he had initially attributed to dietary changes. He also reported unintentional weight loss of approximately 8 pounds over that interval, though his appetite had remained relatively preserved. He denied overt rectal bleeding but noted blood-streaked stools on multiple occasions. He denied tenesmus, rectal urgency, or frank melena.

He underwent colonoscopy performed by gastroenterology, which revealed a partially obstructing mass in the sigmoid colon. Multiple endoscopic biopsies were obtained. He was subsequently referred for cross-sectional imaging. Staging CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast demonstrated a primary sigmoid colon mass measuring 4.5 x 3.8 cm with mesorectal fat stranding and involvement of pericolonic lymph nodes (at least four nodes, largest 1.4 cm). The liver demonstrated four hypodense lesions in a bilobar distribution: a dominant lesion in segment VI measuring 3.8 x 3.1 cm, an additional lesion in segment II measuring 1.9 x 1.6 cm, a lesion in segment IV measuring 2.2 x 1.8 cm, and a smaller lesion in segment VII measuring 1.1 x 0.9 cm. No pulmonary nodules, peritoneal implants, adrenal lesions, or osseous disease were identified. A PET-CT performed subsequently confirmed FDG-avid disease at all hepatic sites and the primary sigmoid mass (SUVmax 14.2 at the primary), with no occult sites of distant disease beyond the liver identified.

Pathologic confirmation was obtained via CT-guided biopsy of the dominant hepatic lesion in segment VI, which showed metastatic moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma, morphologically and immunohistochemically consistent with a colorectal primary (CDX2+, CK20+, CK7-). The colonoscopic biopsies of the sigmoid mass demonstrated moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma with glandular architecture, intraluminal dirty necrosis, and intact mismatch repair proteins by IHC (MLH1+, MSH2+, MSH6+, PMS2+), confirming MMR-proficient/microsatellite stable status.

Extended RAS/RAF testing was performed on the primary tumor tissue, revealing a KRAS exon 2, codon 12, p.G12D mutation (VAF 42.3%). NRAS exons 2, 3, and 4 were wild-type. BRAF V600E was wild-type. HER2 was not amplified (copy number 2.1). MSI by PCR confirmed microsatellite stability. TMB was 6.2 mutations per megabase (low). A comprehensive 500-gene NGS panel additionally identified TP53 p.R248W, APC p.Q1367* (truncating), SMAD4 p.R361H, and PIK3CA p.E545K as co-occurring mutations, along with MYC amplification and CDKN2A homozygous deletion as copy number alterations. No targetable fusions were detected.

Tumor markers at time of diagnosis: CEA 187.4 ng/mL (markedly elevated; normal <2.5 ng/mL in non-smokers), CA 19-9 412 U/mL (markedly elevated; normal <37 U/mL).

His case was reviewed at multidisciplinary gastrointestinal tumor board prior to today's consultation. The hepatic surgery team noted that the bilobar hepatic metastatic burden is not immediately resectable but may be technically convertible to resectability if adequate tumor downsizing can be achieved with systemic therapy. The primary sigmoid mass is causing partial colonic obstruction; colorectal surgery was consulted and determined that diverting colostomy is not required at this time given adequate fecal passage on a high-fiber diet. The patient was advised to monitor closely for signs of complete obstruction.

Mr. Kowalski presents today to discuss results, treatment options, and to initiate systemic therapy planning.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Reports approximately 8-pound weight loss over the past 4-5 months. Mild fatigue, manageable. Denies fever, night sweats, or chills.
Gastrointestinal: Intermittent left lower quadrant cramping. Altered bowel habits as described above (constipation alternating with loose stools). Occasional blood-streaked stools. Denies nausea, vomiting, dysphagia, or odynophagia. No jaundice, dark urine, or acholic stools (LFTs within normal limits as per referral labs).
Respiratory: Denies cough, dyspnea, or hemoptysis.
Cardiovascular: Denies chest pain, palpitations, or lower extremity edema. Hypertension managed as below.
Neurological: Denies focal weakness, numbness, or tingling. No headache or visual changes.
Musculoskeletal: Denies bone pain or arthralgias.
Genitourinary: Denies urinary frequency, dysuria, or hematuria.
Skin: No rash or skin changes.
Endocrine/Metabolic: Type 2 diabetes mellitus, well-controlled per patient report. Denies polydipsia, polyuria.
Psychiatric: Reports significant anxiety and adjustment distress since diagnosis, though he remains highly motivated to pursue treatment. Denies suicidal ideation.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY

1. Hypertension – diagnosed approximately 10 years ago; well-controlled on lisinopril 10 mg daily at baseline; most recent blood pressure readings reportedly 130-138/80-88 mmHg at primary care visits
2. Hyperlipidemia – managed with atorvastatin 20 mg daily; last LDL reportedly 88 mg/dL
3. Type 2 diabetes mellitus – on metformin 1000 mg twice daily; HbA1c 6.8% per records; no microvascular complications documented
4. Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases – as above (newly diagnosed)

No prior malignancies. No prior abdominal or pelvic surgeries. No prior radiation therapy.

---

FAMILY HISTORY

Father: Died at age 74 from myocardial infarction. No known history of gastrointestinal malignancy.
Mother: Alive at age 82; hypertension, osteoarthritis.
Siblings: One sister, age 55, healthy. No family history of colorectal cancer, Lynch syndrome, familial adenomatous polyposis, or other hereditary cancer syndromes.
Children: Two adult children; no known medical issues.
No known family history of breast, ovarian, pancreatic, or other GI cancers.

Given the MMR-proficient/MSS status and the absence of a family history consistent with Lynch syndrome, hereditary CRC syndrome is felt to be unlikely, though genetic counseling referral was offered and discussed with the patient.

---

SOCIAL HISTORY

Occupation: High school history teacher; currently working full-time without limitation.
Smoking: Former smoker; 20 pack-year history; quit 8 years ago.
Alcohol: Occasional social alcohol use (estimated 2-3 drinks per week). Denies heavy or binge drinking.
Illicit drugs: Denies.
Living situation: Lives with his wife in a single-story home; two adult children living nearby.
Support system: Good social support from wife and family. Reports wife will be accompanying him to treatment visits.
Exercise: Walks 2-3 miles per day; reports decreased activity over the past 2 months due to fatigue and abdominal discomfort.
Diet: High-fiber diet implemented on surgical recommendation given partial colonic obstruction.
Insurance: Commercial health insurance with oncology coverage confirmed.
Advance directive: Not yet completed; will be discussed and referred to palliative care team.

---

ALLERGIES

NKDA (No Known Drug Allergies)

---

CURRENT MEDICATIONS

1. Lisinopril 10 mg orally daily (hypertension)
2. Atorvastatin 20 mg orally at bedtime (hyperlipidemia)
3. Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily with meals (type 2 diabetes mellitus)
4. Aspirin 81 mg orally daily (cardiovascular prophylaxis)
5. Over-the-counter multivitamin daily
6. Docusate sodium 100 mg orally twice daily (recently started for constipation)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital signs: Temperature 98.1°F, heart rate 78 bpm (regular), blood pressure 138/88 mmHg (right arm, sitting), respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, oxygen saturation 97% on room air, weight 83.2 kg, height 174 cm, BMI 27.4 kg/m2.

General: Well-appearing, alert and oriented, in no acute distress. Mildly anxious but engaged and asking appropriate questions. Well-nourished.

HEENT: Anicteric sclerae. Mucous membranes moist. No oral lesions. No lymphadenopathy in the neck.

Lymph nodes: No palpable cervical, supraclavicular, axillary, or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema bilaterally.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, rhonchi, or crackles.

Abdomen: Mildly distended. Soft. Mild tenderness to deep palpation in the left lower quadrant without rebound or guarding. No palpable mass appreciated on exam. Liver edge palpable approximately 2 cm below the right costal margin; non-tender. Spleen not palpable. Bowel sounds present and normoactive. No shifting dullness or fluid wave.

Musculoskeletal: No bony tenderness to palpation over the spine, ribs, or pelvis. Full range of motion in major joints.

Neurological: Alert and oriented x3. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Motor strength 5/5 in all four extremities. Sensation to light touch intact distally in upper and lower extremities. Deep tendon reflexes 2+ and symmetric. Gait steady and normal.

Skin: No jaundice, rash, or suspicious lesions. No palmar erythema or spider angiomata.

Rectal: Deferred to colorectal surgery.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

CBC: WBC 6.8 x 10^9/L, hemoglobin 11.9 g/dL (mild normocytic anemia, consistent with anemia of chronic disease/malignancy), platelets 334 x 10^9/L.
CMP: Na 138 mEq/L, K 4.1 mEq/L, Cl 102 mEq/L, CO2 24 mEq/L, BUN 16 mg/dL, creatinine 1.02 mg/dL (eGFR 74 mL/min/1.73m2, CKD stage G2), glucose 122 mg/dL. LFTs: AST 31 U/L, ALT 38 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 112 U/L (mildly elevated, likely from hepatic metastatic burden), total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL, albumin 3.8 g/dL.
Coagulation: INR 1.1, PT 12.4 sec.
Lipase: Normal.
Magnesium: 1.9 mg/dL.
HbA1c: 6.8% (per outside records).
CEA: 187.4 ng/mL (markedly elevated).
CA 19-9: 412 U/mL (markedly elevated).
Echo not yet ordered; baseline cardiac evaluation deferred given no cardiovascular symptoms, though may be considered if clinical concern arises during treatment.

---

IMAGING RESULTS (REVIEWED)

CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast (staging, recent): 4.5 x 3.8 cm sigmoid colon primary mass. Four bilobar hepatic lesions as described above (largest in segment VI at 3.8 x 3.1 cm). No pulmonary nodules. No peritoneal implants. No adrenal or osseous metastases.

PET-CT (staging, recent): FDG-avid sigmoid primary (SUVmax 14.2); FDG-avid hepatic metastases in segments II, IV, VI, and VII (SUVmax range 8.7-12.4). No FDG-avid pulmonary, peritoneal, osseous, or nodal disease beyond the regional hepatic region.

Multidisciplinary tumor board imaging review summary: hepatic disease is bilobar but limited in number of lesions (four total). With adequate tumor response to systemic therapy, hepatic resection/ablation may become technically feasible. The proximity of the segment II lesion to the left hepatic vein requires careful monitoring.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Mr. Kowalski is a 58-year-old male with newly diagnosed Stage IV (cT4aN2bM1a, AJCC 8th edition) left-sided sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases. Molecular profile: KRAS p.G12D mutant (pathogenic, oncogenic driver), NRAS/BRAF wild-type, HER2 non-amplified, MMR-proficient/MSS, TMB-low (6.2 mut/Mb). Co-mutations include TP53, APC, SMAD4, and PIK3CA. ECOG PS 1. His case was reviewed in multidisciplinary tumor board.

IMPRESSION:
This is a challenging but potentially partially convertible presentation. The bilobar hepatic metastatic burden (four lesions, largest 3.8 cm) is not immediately resectable, but the hepatic surgery team believes that meaningful tumor response may render at least the hepatic component surgically addressable. The primary sigmoid mass is causing partial obstruction but is being managed conservatively for now. The KRAS G12D mutation categorically precludes anti-EGFR therapy (cetuximab, panitumumab); no targeted therapy is currently FDA-approved for KRAS-mutant colorectal cancer in the first-line setting. Given MSS status, immunotherapy with checkpoint inhibitors (e.g., pembrolizumab, nivolumab) is not indicated here. TMB-low further supports the limited utility of immune checkpoint blockade in this case.

IMPORTANT MOLECULAR CONSIDERATIONS:
The SMAD4 p.R361H mutation is noted. SMAD4 loss has been associated with a pattern of systemic metastasis and, in some retrospective analyses, with inferior outcomes with anti-VEGF therapy, though this does not preclude bevacizumab use and does not alter standard first-line recommendations at this time. The PIK3CA p.E545K mutation may be relevant for future clinical trial eligibility. The KRAS G12D mutation, while not targetable with currently approved agents for CRC (sotorasib and adagrasib have shown limited activity in colon cancer compared to lung cancer due to distinct downstream signaling contexts), is an area of active clinical investigation, and I will maintain awareness of relevant clinical trial opportunities for future lines of therapy.

PLAN:

1. First-line systemic therapy: Initiate mFOLFOX6 (oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2 IV day 1, leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV day 1, 5-FU 400 mg/m2 IV bolus day 1 then 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours) plus bevacizumab 5 mg/kg IV day 1, every 14 days. This represents the standard first-line backbone for KRAS/NRAS/BRAF wild-type...or RAS-mutant left-sided mCRC in a patient with no contraindications to bevacizumab. The intent is to achieve sufficient tumor response for potential hepatic resection consideration.
   - Bevacizumab will be held for cycle 1 given that the CT-guided liver biopsy was performed approximately 10 days prior (wound healing consideration; will initiate with cycle 2 given standard 4-week hold recommendation; however, given 10-day interval, we will administer beva starting cycle 2 per discussion with surgical team).
   - Oxaliplatin neurotoxicity counseling provided extensively: cumulative peripheral sensory neuropathy is the dose-limiting toxicity; patient instructed to avoid cold exposure and cold beverages during and for 48-72 hours after oxaliplatin infusion to prevent cold-induced dysesthesias. Gloves and warm beverages advised. Baseline peripheral sensation documented as intact.

2. Blood pressure management: Baseline BP today is 138/88 mmHg, which is modestly above his typical controlled range. This should be addressed proactively given anticipated bevacizumab-related hypertension. I have communicated with his primary care physician to optimize antihypertensive management prior to initiation of bevacizumab. Lisinopril will remain at 10 mg daily for now; we will monitor BP at each visit and dose-escalate or add agents as needed. Patient educated on home BP monitoring and instructed to maintain a log.

3. Bowel/surgical management: Colorectal surgery is aware of the partial obstruction from the primary tumor. The patient is managing adequately on a high-fiber diet. No diverting colostomy planned at this time. Patient has been instructed to report urgently if he develops signs of complete obstruction (inability to pass gas or stool, severe abdominal distension, intractable vomiting). If obstruction worsens, surgical or endoscopic stenting will be considered before chemotherapy can safely continue.

4. Supportive medications and antiemetics: Premedication with dexamethasone 12 mg IV and ondansetron 8 mg IV prior to each treatment. Metoclopramide 10 mg PO q6h PRN as rescue antiemetic for the following 48 hours. Oral cryotherapy (ice chips in mouth during 5-FU bolus) to reduce risk of mucositis. 5-FU pump teaching provided; patient is a good candidate for ambulatory pump management.

5. Glycemic monitoring: Will monitor blood glucose at treatment visits given corticosteroid use with premedication. Metformin to be held on IV contrast days per radiology protocol. HbA1c to be followed quarterly.

6. Hepatic resection pathway: Will restage with CT at approximately 6-8 cycles (approximately 3 months from treatment initiation). Will re-present to multidisciplinary tumor board for reassessment of hepatic resectability at that time. Our goal is to achieve sufficient downsizing of the hepatic metastases to allow an R0 resection with adequate future liver remnant. If conversion to resectability is achieved, we will coordinate with hepatobiliary surgery for a combined or staged resection approach (primary sigmoid mass + liver metastases). The patient understands that this remains an aspirational goal and that outcomes are not guaranteed.

7. Tumor markers: CEA and CA 19-9 will be checked at each cycle. Baseline CEA 187.4 ng/mL, CA 19-9 412 U/mL serve as reference.

8. Baseline labs and monitoring: CBC, CMP including LFTs prior to each cycle. PERT (port placement referral) discussed; patient agreeable to port placement for vascular access prior to cycle 1.

9. Referral: Palliative care consulted for advance care planning, goals of care, and symptom management support (not a substitute for active oncologic treatment; patient understands this distinction). Social work referral placed given significant psychosocial adjustment distress. Genetic counseling offered; patient declined at this time but will be reconsidered.

10. Clinical trial awareness: Patient made aware of ongoing and emerging KRAS G12D-specific clinical trials for colorectal cancer. At this time no applicable first-line KRAS-targeted trial was identified, but we will continue to monitor for trial availability, particularly for later lines. Registry enrollment discussion planned for the future.

11. Return to clinic: Patient to return for cycle 1 of mFOLFOX6 in approximately 1 week following port placement. Follow-up appointment for cycle 1 on treatment day.

The risks, benefits, and alternatives to the proposed regimen were discussed in detail with the patient and his wife. Specific adverse effects reviewed included: peripheral sensory neuropathy (cumulative, potentially dose-limiting), bevacizumab-related hypertension and rare but serious risk of gastrointestinal perforation, wound healing impairment, arterial thromboembolic events (MI, stroke), venous thromboembolic events, proteinuria, and rare posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome; 5-FU-related mucositis, diarrhea, myelosuppression, and rare cardiotoxicity and DPD deficiency-related severe toxicity (DPD testing offered; patient declined). Questions were answered at length. Patient is highly motivated, engaged, and in agreement with the proposed plan. He expressed a clear desire for aggressive, active treatment.

PROGNOSIS DISCUSSION: A frank but compassionate discussion was held regarding prognosis. Stage IV colorectal cancer with synchronous hepatic metastases, particularly in the context of a KRAS mutation, carries a guarded prognosis; median overall survival in the first-line setting with FOLFOX/bevacizumab is in the range of 20-25 months from published trial data, though outcomes vary substantially and a subset of patients with convertible disease who achieve hepatic resection may have significantly better outcomes. I emphasized that the goal at this time is to maximize tumor response to open the door to a potential surgical cure attempt, while preserving quality of life. This was understood and accepted by the patient.

Electronically signed by: [Attending Medical Oncologist]
Specialty: Medical Oncology / Gastrointestinal Oncology
Dictated and reviewed.
